name: Anishinaabe
traditionalTerritories: Great Lakes region spanning Ontario, Manitoba, Saskatchewan, Michigan, Wisconsin, Minnesota, and North Dakota. Extends from Lake Superior to Lake Huron, the Ottawa River valley, and the Lake of the Woods region.
culturalNotes: Anishinaabe is a broad umbrella term for a group of culturally and linguistically related peoples. The Anishinaabe Confederacy historically included the Ojibwe, Odawa, and Potawatomi (Three Fires Confederacy), along with related nations such as the Algonquin and Mississauga. Contemporary Anishinaabe communities are located across the Great Lakes basin and northern Ontario and Manitoba. Anishinaabe cosmology includes the Manitou (spiritual beings), the seven grandfather teachings (Nibwaakaawin, Zaagi'idiwin, Minaadendamowin, Aakode'ewin, Gwayakwaadiziwin, Dabaadendiziwin, Debwewin), and a rich oral tradition maintained by storytellers and ceremony keepers.
heritageSkillRelevance: Birchbark canoe construction (the most developed canoe-building tradition in North America)
Wild rice (manoomin) harvesting, processing, and preparation — a sacred and politically significant food
Maple sugar and syrup production — sugar bush camp was a major seasonal community event
Birchbark baskets, containers, and wiigiwaam (dome shelter) construction
Quillwork (porcupine quill embroidery on birchbark, leather, and fabric)
Beadwork using wampum and later glass trade beads
Woodland plant knowledge — medicine plants, edible plants, dye plants
Fishing using nets, weirs, spears, and hooks in the Great Lakes system
Snowshoe construction for woodland and open terrain travel
communityAuthorizedResources: Ojibwe Cultural Foundation (OCF) — www.ojibweculture.ca — M'Chigeeng First Nation
Anishinaabemowin Teg — language and cultural revitalization programs
Great Lakes Indian Fish and Wildlife Commission (GLIFWC) publications on manoomin
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Wild rice (manoomin) harvesting has specific cultural protocols at Level 2 (contextually shared, educational framing with referral to Anishinaabe sources). The ceremonial aspects of manoomin harvesting and the Seven Grandfather Teachings in full ceremony context are Level 3. Midewiwin (Grand Medicine Society) ceremonies are Level 4 — never include.
